claim_verdicts.slide: Comparison Table — Micro-Batch vs. Continuous vs. Flink
claim_verdicts.source_excerpt: achieving end-to-end latencies as low as 100 milliseconds and exactly-once fault-tolerance guarantees

claim_verdicts.slide: Comparison Table — Micro-Batch vs. Continuous vs. Flink
claim_verdicts.source_excerpt: Continuous processing is a new, experimental streaming execution mode... that enables low (~1 ms) end-to-end latency with at-least-once fault-tolerance guarantees.

claim_verdicts.slide: Comparison Table — Micro-Batch vs. Continuous vs. Flink
claim_verdicts.bullet_text: Supported operations: Map-like only (select, filter, map); Sources: Kafka, Rate only
claim_verdicts.source_excerpt: Only map-like Dataset/DataFrame operations are supported in continuous mode, that is, only projections (select, map, flatMap, mapPartitions, etc.) and selections (where, filter, etc.).
claim_verdicts.note: Source explicitly lists map-like limitation. 'Kafka, Rate only' for sources is standard Spark documentation knowledge, not contradicted by this source.

claim_verdicts.slide: Comparison Table — Micro-Batch vs. Continuous vs. Flink
claim_verdicts.bullet_text: Task failure handling: No auto retry — query stops
claim_verdicts.source_excerpt: There are currently no automatic retries of failed tasks. Any failure will lead to the query being stopped and it needs to be manually restarted from the checkpoint.

claim_verdicts.slide: Comparison Table — Micro-Batch vs. Continuous vs. Flink
claim_verdicts.bullet_text: State access: Local, often in-memory state
claim_verdicts.source_excerpt: Tasks perform all computations by accessing local, often in-memory, state yielding very low processing latencies.
claim_verdicts.note: PREVIOUSLY FLAGGED in v1 as MISREPRESENTED (sub-millisecond and RocksDB not in source). The v2 slide bullet is NOW FIXED — it says 'Local, often in-memory state' which matches the source verbatim. HOWEVER, the speaker notes (lines 847-852) still contain the exact unsupported language: 'local state access (RocksDB on each task manager)' and 'sub-millisecond latency'. The source says 'often in-memory' (not RocksDB) and 'very low processing latencies' (not sub-millisecond). See flagged_issues.

claim_verdicts.bullet_text: Only map-like operations — no aggregations, no joins, no stateful processing
claim_verdicts.source_excerpt: Only map-like Dataset/DataFrame operations are supported in continuous mode... All SQL functions are supported except aggregation functions.

claim_verdicts.bullet_text: Three blockers: (1) only map-like ops, (2) two separate checkpointing systems needed, (3) significant engine reimplementation
claim_verdicts.source_excerpt: The SPIP evaluated extending the experimental Continuous Processing mode... concluded it was inadequate due to three key limitations: only map operations with no stateful processing, would require maintaining two separate checkpointing systems, and significant portions of the engine would need reimplementation.

claim_verdicts.bullet_text: Long-running tasks with no automatic retries made it fragile in production
claim_verdicts.source_excerpt: The continuous processing engine launches multiple long-running tasks... There are currently no automatic retries of failed tasks. Any failure will lead to the query being stopped.

claim_verdicts.bullet_text: Each micro-batch writes separate output files — 2,880 file batches/day with 30s trigger; _spark_metadata log grows unboundedly and can OOM the driver; Delta Lake auto-compaction available
claim_verdicts.source_excerpt: Each micro-batch commits separate output files, leading to an accumulation of many small files... The size of metadata files grows with time... can reach up to 10's of GB's. Auto compaction combines small files within Delta table partitions.
claim_verdicts.note: 2,880 = 86400/30 is correct arithmetic. Source confirms metadata growth and auto-compaction.

claim_verdicts.slide: The Small-File Problem; Table-Format Sinks — Delta Lake and Iceberg
claim_verdicts.bullet_text: Each batch produces a new snapshot with small data files; run periodic compaction (rewriteDataFiles every 1-4 hours); expire old snapshots; requires explicit maintenance
claim_verdicts.source_excerpt: Each write to an Iceberg table creates a new snapshot... Snapshots accumulate until they are expired by the expireSnapshots operation. Regularly expiring snapshots is recommended... Tables receiving continuous streaming ingestion benefit from compaction every 1-4 hours.

claim_verdicts.slide: State Store Growth Under Skewed Keys
claim_verdicts.bullet_text: Skewed keys cause uneven state distribution — one executor holds disproportionate state; HDFS-backed store causes JVM GC pauses and potential OOM; RocksDB must set boundedMemoryUsage=true
claim_verdicts.source_excerpt: large number of state objects puts memory pressure on the JVM causing high GC pauses... If left unbounded, RocksDB memory usage across multiple instances could grow indefinitely and potentially cause OOM... cap RocksDB memory usage by setting spark.sql.streaming.stateStore.rocksdb.boundedMemoryUsage config to true... identify potential state skew (uneven distribution of keys across partitions).
claim_verdicts.note: Source covers all three sub-claims: state skew (uneven distribution), JVM GC pressure with HDFS store, and RocksDB boundedMemoryUsage requirement. All three elements are present in the source text.

claim_verdicts.slide: State Store Growth Under Skewed Keys
claim_verdicts.bullet_text: Switch to RocksDB with explicit memory bounds
claim_verdicts.source_excerpt: Rather than keeping the state in the JVM memory, this solution uses RocksDB to efficiently manage the state in the native memory and the local disk.

claim_verdicts.slide: Checkpoint Compatibility — What You Can and Cannot Change
claim_verdicts.bullet_text: spark.sql.shuffle.partitions is frozen because state is hash-partitioned — changing it would orphan state; Stateful operation changes not allowed between restarts
claim_verdicts.source_excerpt: spark.sql.shuffle.partitions: 'This is due to the physical partitioning of state: state is partitioned via applying hash function to key, hence the number of partitions for state should be unchanged.' Also: 'Any changes (additions, deletions, or schema modifications) to the stateful operations of a streaming query are not allowed between restarts.'
claim_verdicts.note: Slide says 'orphan state' — source says partitions must be unchanged because state is hash-partitioned. 'Orphan' is an accurate characterization: keys hash to different partitions, making existing state entries unreachable.

claim_verdicts.slide: Checkpoint Compatibility — What You Can and Cannot Change
claim_verdicts.bullet_text: Safe changes include adding/removing filters and changing rate limits or trigger intervals
claim_verdicts.source_excerpt: Adding or removing filters... Addition, deletion, and modification of rate limits is allowed... Trigger intervals [are safe modifications].

claim_verdicts.slide: Table-Format Sinks — Delta Lake and Iceberg
claim_verdicts.bullet_text: Transaction log guarantees exactly-once even with concurrent writers; built-in auto-compaction via OPTIMIZE; Pitfall: deleted checkpoint + same appId causes silently ignored writes
claim_verdicts.source_excerpt: The transaction log enables Delta Lake to guarantee exactly-once processing, even when there are other streams or batch queries running concurrently... If you delete the streaming checkpoint and restart the query with a new checkpoint, you must provide a different appId; otherwise, writes from the restarted query will be ignored... Auto compaction combines small files.

claim_verdicts.bullet_text: streams are unbounded tables; users write declarative queries that the engine automatically incrementalizes
claim_verdicts.source_excerpt: It is a purely declarative API based on automatically incrementalizing a static relational query (expressed using SQL or DataFrames).

claim_verdicts.bullet_text: two-log WAL protocol (OffsetSeqLog + CommitLog) guarantees exactly-once with replayable sources and idempotent sinks
claim_verdicts.source_excerpt: The engine uses checkpointing and write-ahead logs to record the offset range... using replayable sources and idempotent sinks, Structured Streaming can ensure end-to-end exactly-once semantics. The system maintains two logs: OffsetSeqLog and CommitLog.

claim_verdicts.bullet_text: watermark = max(event_time) - threshold; bounds state growth and controls the completeness-vs-latency tradeoff
claim_verdicts.source_excerpt: the watermark for C is max(C) - t_C, that is, t_C seconds before the maximum event time seen so far in C.

flagged_issues.location: Comparison Table — Micro-Batch vs. Continuous vs. Flink (speaker notes, lines 847-852)
flagged_issues.issue: The slide BULLET for claim:sss:flink_per_event:018 was correctly fixed from v1 — it now says 'Local, often in-memory state' matching the source. However, the speaker notes for this slide still contain the EXACT unsupported claims flagged in v1: (1) 'local state access (RocksDB on each task manager)' — source says 'local, often in-memory, state', NOT 'RocksDB on each task manager'; (2) 'sub-millisecond latency' — source says 'very low processing latencies', NOT 'sub-millisecond'. The lecturer reading these notes aloud would make the same unsupported claims that were flagged in v1.
flagged_issues.suggested_fix: In speaker notes (around line 848), change 'with local state access (RocksDB on each task manager). It achieves sub-millisecond latency' to 'with local, often in-memory state access. It achieves very low per-event latency'. This aligns notes with the source and prevents the lecturer from making unsupported claims.

v1_regression_check.v1_issue: MISREPRESENTED — slide bullet said 'sub-millisecond' and 'RocksDB', neither supported by source
v1_regression_check.v2_bullet_status: FIXED — bullet now says 'Local, often in-memory state', matching source
v1_regression_check.v2_notes_status: NOT FIXED — speaker notes still contain 'sub-millisecond' and 'RocksDB on each task manager'
v1_regression_check.overall: Partially fixed. Bullet is correct but speaker notes carry the same unsupported language.

summary: Full adversarial re-scan of Sections G-I: 18 claim references checked across 8 content slides. 18 out of 18 claim-tagged BULLET references are VERIFIED against their sources. The previously flagged claim:sss:flink_per_event:018 bullet is now correctly worded. However, the speaker notes for that slide still contain the exact unsupported language from v1 ('sub-millisecond latency', 'RocksDB on each task manager'). Verdict is NEEDS_REVISION due to the speaker notes containing unsupported claims that a lecturer would read aloud. One issue to fix.
